issues.claim: What the saṁyamī sees fully, others do not register; what others register fully, the muni does not see
issues.objection: Lexically accurate but compresses two distinct technical terms (saṁyamī, muniḥ) into a single perceptual archetype. Shankara reads them as pointing to two stages on the same path — saṁyamī = the practitioner of restraint, muni = the realised one. The engineering layer flattens this into a single 'senior engineer' figure, which is acceptable simplification at the operational layer (the engineering analog cannot describe a saṁyamī-vs-muni stage gap with engineering specificity) but loses the chapter's careful technical vocabulary about disciplined-aspirant-vs-realised. The source pack flags this in the disagreements list. Acceptable.

notes: The engineering layer correctly preserves: (a) the awake-to-X / asleep-to-Y inversion structure verbatim — what the saṁyamī sees fully, others do not see at all; what others see fully, the muni does not register; (b) the Shankara reading that the inversion is paramārtha-sat (substrate) vs vyāvahārika (surface of differentiated forms) — the engineering analog renders this as 'technical truth' (substrate) vs 'political weather' (surface of meeting performance), which is a faithful operational mapping; (c) the perceptual-aperture framing — 'same scene; redirected aperture' is verbatim Shankara's structural reading; (d) the falsifiability clause that explicitly warns against the misread (most quiet engineers are simply unprepared); (e) the counter-example acknowledging cases where surface IS substrate (personnel decisions). Pass.
